steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Central Ideas and Details question. The passage calls the energy ladder model "reductive" after describing how Nygaard's study showed fuel choice is more complex. The correct answer must explain why that label fits — specifically, what the model oversimplifies and what the study reveals instead.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The energy ladder asserts that households only consider one aspect of fuel when making fuel choices, but Nygaard's study showed that households ignore that characteristic of fuel and evaluate several other factors instead.: The second half is wrong. Nygaard's study shows households consider multiple factors, including local fuel prices; the passage does not say households ignore income, only that income alone does not explain fuel choice. ✗
  • Choice B — The energy ladder holds that the adoption of one type of fuel means that another type of fuel must be displaced, but Nygaard's study showed that several different fuel types are typically used in equal proportion.: Misrepresents both the model (which is about income, not displacement) and the study (which never says fuels are used in equal proportion). ✗
  • Choice C — The energy ladder assumes that the same factor influences household fuel choice in all locations, but Nygaard's study showed that fuel choice in Guatemala is influenced by different factors than is fuel choice in Costa Rica.: The passage does not compare Guatemala's influencing factors to Costa Rica's — it only mentions Costa Rica as context for the fuel-use shift. ✗
  • Choice D — The energy ladder attributes household fuel choice primarily to a single characteristic of households, but Nygaard's study showed that multiple circumstances can affect household fuel choice.: Accurately captures both sides: the ladder reduces choice to one factor (income), while Nygaard found choice is heterogeneous and influenced by several factors. ✓
That is the correct answer.
